message.content: {"items":[{"title":"International Social Survey Programme: Family and Changing Gender Roles III - ISSP 2002","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.11842","justification":"International Social Survey Programme (ISSP) 2002 focuses on family life, marriage, and changing gender roles. The study includes many of the requested countries (e.g., Australia, Austria, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Cyprus, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Great Britain, Hungary, Israel, Italy, Japan, Latvia, New Zealand, Norway, Philippines, Poland, Russian Federation, Slovenia, Spain, South Africa, Switzerland, United States) and falls in the early 2000s. It directly addresses social behaviour, family life and marriage."},{"title":"International Social Survey Programme: Family and Changing Gender Roles IV - ISSP 2012","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.11847","justification":"Although fielded around 2012, parts of data collection in some countries touch the late 2000s. It covers attitudes to family life, gender roles, partnership and marriage, with extensive country coverage including many of the listed countries. It is relevant to social behaviour and family life, though it slightly extends beyond the 2000s timeframe."},{"title":"European Social Survey 2002 (Round 1)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.12253","justification":"ESS Round 1 (2002) includes multiple European countries from the list (e.g. Austria, Czech Republic, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hungary, Israel, Italy, Norway, Poland, Spain, Switzerland). Core modules include social trust, social behaviour, and attitudes, and rotating modules touch on family, work and related values, making it relevant for social behaviour and social stratification."},{"title":"European Social Survey 2004 (Round 2)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.12254","justification":"ESS Round 2 (2004) covers several of the requested European countries and contains rich attitudinal data on social behaviour, social inequalities and groupings, and aspects of family and work life. It is fully within the 2000s."},{"title":"European Social Survey 2006 (Round 3)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.12255","justification":"ESS Round 3 (2006) again includes many target countries and measures social attitudes and behaviours. Rotating modules (e.g., on the timing of life events, work–family balance, and wellbeing) allow analysis of family life, partnership, and social stratification during the 2000s."},{"title":"European Social Survey 2008 (Round 4)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.12256","justification":"ESS Round 4 (2008) provides cross-national attitudinal data for several of the listed European countries. It includes variables on family, social contacts, group membership, and social stratification dimensions such as education, class, and income, all situated in the late 2000s."},{"title":"European Social Survey 2010 (Round 5)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.12257","justification":"ESS Round 5 (2010) is just beyond the 2000s but captures continuities from that decade. It covers many of the European countries you listed and includes modules on family, work, social trust, group memberships, and socio‑economic stratification. It is relevant for understanding late-2000s patterns and trends."},{"title":"ISSP 2001: Social Networks and Social Support (Germany)","url_or_doi":"https://dbk.gesis.org/dbksearch/sdesc2.asp?no=3450&db=e&doi=10.4232/1.3450","justification":"This ISSP module focuses on social networks, social support and social behaviour. The German dataset hosted by GESIS is within the 2000s and includes variables on household and family context, making it relevant to social behaviour, social stratification, and aspects of family life in Germany."},{"title":"ISSP 2000: Environment II (selected countries)","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.11834","justification":"While the main focus is environmental attitudes, the ISSP 2000 dataset includes standard socio‑demographic and household/family variables and covers several of the requested countries. These background variables allow analyses of social stratification and groupings in the context of environmental behaviour in the early 2000s."},{"title":"ISSP 2006: Role of Government IV","url_or_doi":"https://doi.org/10.4232/1.11747","justification":"This survey, fielded in 2006, examines attitudes toward government, welfare state, and social policies, including family and social security issues. It includes many of the countries you listed and has detailed socio‑demographic information relevant to social stratification and groupings during the mid‑2000s."}]}
